title: Module 1 quiz: What an agent actually is

questions.prompt: What's the cleanest way to distinguish a workflow from an agent?

choices.text: Who decides the next step: in a workflow you (the developer) decide the sequence and the LLM fills in content; in an agent, the LLM picks the next step (which tool, which arguments, when to stop). The first is predictable and cheap; the second is flexible and expensive.

questions.explanation: Workflows = developer chooses steps. Agents = model chooses steps. Workflows are simpler, faster, cheaper, more reliable; agents are flexible for problems where the path genuinely depends on intermediate results. Default to workflow; promote only when needed.

questions.prompt: What's the universal pattern every agent framework reduces to?

choices.text: A loop: call model → execute requested tools → append results to conversation → repeat until model signals done or step cap fires. Every framework (OpenAI Agents SDK, LangGraph, AutoGen, Anthropic tool use) is a wrapper around this loop.

questions.explanation: The agent loop is the ReAct pattern: reason, act, observe, repeat. Frameworks add ergonomics around it — the underlying mechanic is unchanged. You can implement an agent in 30 lines of code without any framework.

questions.prompt: Your agent ran overnight in a fetch-retry-fetch loop, burning a 4-figure bill. What was missing?

choices.text: A step cap (maxSteps). Without one, a buggy agent or adversarial input loops forever. The pattern: cap iterations, treat hitting the cap as an explicit error, escalate or return partial answer. Pair with per-tool timeouts and daily spend caps for layered safety.

questions.explanation: Step caps are non-negotiable. They convert 'unbounded loop' into 'explicit error to handle.' Combine with timeouts, approvals on destructive tools, and daily cost caps for full safety.

questions.prompt: Roughly how many tools should a single production agent have?

choices.text: 5–15 is the sweet spot. Fewer than 5 means a workflow could do the job; more than 15 means token cost balloons and selection quality drops. Past ~20 tools, consider splitting into specialized agents or gating tools by route.

questions.explanation: Tool count drives both prompt size (every definition lives in input on every call) and selection complexity (more choices = worse selection). Split into specialized agents or use route-based tool gating once you exceed ~20 tools.

questions.prompt: You're routing customer messages with an agent that always calls `classify_intent` then `route_to_team`. Cost is 4× a workflow equivalent. Right move?

choices.text: Replace with a single LLM call returning `{intent, team}` as structured output. The 'agent' is doing fixed work — the agent overhead buys nothing. Reserve agent loops for problems where the path genuinely varies based on intermediate results.

questions.explanation: Most 'agents' in production are actually workflows wearing agent costumes. If the steps are predictable, drop to a workflow or single LLM call. Agents earn their cost only when the value is the system figuring out what to do — not when the path is the same every time.
